raw:
{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.7,
    "R1.2": 0.7,
    "R1.3": 0.7,
    "R1.4": 0.7,
    "R1.5": 0.3,
    "R1.6": 0.3,
    "R1.7": 0.7,
    "RX.1": 1.0,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 1.0,
    "RX.5": 1.0
  },
  "active_set": ["R1.1", "R1.2", "R1.3", "R1.4", "R1.7", "RX.1", "RX.2", "RX.3", "RX.4", "RX.5"],
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "Artifact makes an explicit contribution claim ('first systematic study of token-level recalibration in modern transformer ASR encoders'); evaluating whether that framing is coherent and well-grounded is relevant under almost any reading of the vague delegation.",
    "R1.2": "Several internal consistency issues are detectable—most critically, Hannun et al. (2014) is labeled 'most recent confidence-aware ASR model' while being a 12-year-old citation; logical integrity of the baseline comparison depends on this being accurate.",
    "R1.3": "Quantitative claims (ECE 3.1% vs 5.8%, WER regression < 0.1) are asserted alongside a reference to Figure 3; alignment between those numbers and the stated experimental setup is a natural check under any general review.",
    "R1.4": "The paper explicitly stakes a novelty claim; assessing whether that delta over prior work is accurately characterized—particularly given the suspicious baseline dating—is central to evaluating the contribution.",
    "R1.7": "Citation accuracy is flagged by at least two anomalies: Hannun et al. (2014) described as the 'most recent' method (implausible given the field's pace) and Guo et al. (2017) as 'de facto current standard' without qualification; these are potentially load-bearing misattributions."
  },
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "Are you asking for a technical accuracy and citation check (verifying factual claims, baseline dating, and reference correctness), or for a conceptual contribution and novelty assessment (evaluating whether the claimed delta over prior work is meaningful and well-positioned)?"
}

rationale.R1.1: Artifact makes an explicit contribution claim ('first systematic study of token-level recalibration in modern transformer ASR encoders'); evaluating whether that framing is coherent and well-grounded is relevant under almost any reading of the vague delegation.
rationale.R1.2: Several internal consistency issues are detectable—most critically, Hannun et al. (2014) is labeled 'most recent confidence-aware ASR model' while being a 12-year-old citation; logical integrity of the baseline comparison depends on this being accurate.
rationale.R1.3: Quantitative claims (ECE 3.1% vs 5.8%, WER regression < 0.1) are asserted alongside a reference to Figure 3; alignment between those numbers and the stated experimental setup is a natural check under any general review.
rationale.R1.4: The paper explicitly stakes a novelty claim; assessing whether that delta over prior work is accurately characterized—particularly given the suspicious baseline dating—is central to evaluating the contribution.
rationale.R1.7: Citation accuracy is flagged by at least two anomalies: Hannun et al. (2014) described as the 'most recent' method (implausible given the field's pace) and Guo et al. (2017) as 'de facto current standard' without qualification; these are potentially load-bearing misattributions.

parsed.clarification_question: Are you asking for a technical accuracy and citation check (verifying factual claims, baseline dating, and reference correctness), or for a conceptual contribution and novelty assessment (evaluating whether the claimed delta over prior work is meaningful and well-positioned)?
